REN-007 Adjusted Analyses Report

1 Population Summary

This report summarizes the evaluable ITT1FL = "Y" population and presents adjusted efficacy and post-surgery laboratory analyses for Placebo and RBT-1.

1.1 Statistical Analysis

This study was a phase 3, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study evaluating the effect of RBT-1 on reducing the risk of postoperative complications in subjects undergoing cardiac surgery. All analyses in this report were conducted using the Analysis Data Model (ADaM) datasets supplied for study. The primary analytic population was the evaluable intent-to-treat population (ITT1FL = "Y"), defined in the ADaM metadata as randomized subjects with both treatment start and surgery date available. Planned treatment assignment (TRT01P/TRTP) was used to define the comparison groups, placebo and RBT-1.

The ADaM datasets used in these analyses included ADSL for subject-level demographics, treatment assignment, analysis flags, surgery timing, and derived clinical outcomes; ADMH for pre-surgical medical history; ADSC for subject characteristics and baseline clinical factors; and ADLB for laboratory endpoints. Clinical efficacy endpoints evaluated in this report included death (DTHFL), dialysis-requiring acute kidney injury (AKIDIA), 30-day cardiopulmonary readmission (CPRAD30A), ICU days (TICUDYIH), and ICU hours (TICUHRIH). Laboratory endpoints included estimated glomerular filtration rate (GFRECRT), oxygen saturation (OXYSAT), lactic acid (LACTICAC), troponin I (TROPONI), and serum creatinine (SCREAT).

Continuous variables were summarized using means, standard deviations, medians, and quartiles, and categorical variables were summarized using counts and percentages. For unadjusted efficacy comparisons, binary endpoints were compared using Fisher’s exact tests because of the small number of observed events, and continuous ICU duration outcomes were compared using Welch two-sample t-tests and Wilcoxon rank-sum tests. These analyses were used descriptively to characterize treatment-group differences before covariate adjustment.

Adjusted efficacy analyses incorporated pre-surgical medical history information derived from ADMH. Subject-level medical history covariates included the total number of medical history records, the number of distinct body systems represented, the number of cardiac-history terms, and indicators for vascular, metabolic/nutritional, respiratory, renal/urinary, nervous-system, and social-circumstance history. These variables were summarized into a pre-surgical medical history burden score for the main adjusted efficacy models. Binary efficacy endpoints were analyzed using logistic regression, and ICU duration outcomes were analyzed using linear regression on log-transformed values. For continuous outcomes, model coefficients were back-transformed and expressed approximately as percent differences between RBT-1 and placebo.

Baseline subject characteristics were additionally derived from ADSC, including chronic kidney disease, diabetes, hypertension, coronary artery disease, peripheral artery disease, urgency status, heart failure status, NYHA class, and left ventricular ejection fraction. In the primary adjusted laboratory analyses, these variables were represented through a summary burden score. In renal sensitivity analyses, the same ADSC and ADMH components were entered explicitly as individual covariates. Age and sex were obtained from ADSL and included directly in adjusted models where specified.

For laboratory analyses, assessment timing was aligned to surgery date by merging ADLB with SURSDT from ADSL. Postoperative day was calculated relative to surgery rather than treatment start. Two analytic windows were defined. The immediate postoperative window included postoperative days 1 through 7. For single-summary immediate analyses, one “worst” value per subject was selected within this interval, defined as the minimum value for GFRECRT and OXYSAT, and the maximum value for SCREAT, LACTICAC, and TROPONI. The long-term window included the nearest available post-surgical value at or after day 28, prioritizing values closest to day 30; long-term follow-up was available primarily for GFRECRT and SCREAT. These laboratory endpoints were analyzed using linear regression on log-transformed values, adjusted for treatment group, age, sex, pre-surgical medical history burden, subject-characteristic burden, and the corresponding baseline laboratory value when available.

Because renal outcomes were of particular interest, additional sensitivity analyses were performed for serum creatinine and estimated glomerular filtration rate using explicit covariate-by-covariate adjustment. These models included treatment group, age, sex, the corresponding pre-surgical renal laboratory value, ADMH-derived history components, and individual ADSC covariates for chronic kidney disease, diabetes, hypertension, coronary artery disease, peripheral artery disease, urgency, heart failure status, NYHA class III/IV, and left ventricular ejection fraction.

Longitudinal first-week postoperative analyses were also performed for serum creatinine and estimated glomerular filtration rate. For these analyses, one analysis value per subject per postoperative day was retained across postoperative days 1 through 7. Mixed-effects linear models with a random intercept for subject were fit to log-transformed laboratory values. Fixed effects included treatment group, postoperative day, treatment-by-day interaction, age, and the corresponding pre-surgical laboratory value. The main treatment effect represented the average between-group difference over the first postoperative week, whereas the treatment-by-day interaction tested whether postoperative trajectories differed between treatment groups. Adjusted predicted values from these models were used to generate treatment-group trajectory plots.

All hypothesis tests were two-sided and are interpreted descriptively. No multiplicity adjustment was applied for the exploratory analyses presented in this report. All analyses were implemented in R version 4.5.3 using the supplied ADaM datasets.

2.1 Binary Endpoints

Adjusted Binary Efficacy Results
Logistic regression adjusted using pre-surgery medical history summary
Endpoint Model Odds ratio (RBT-1 vs Placebo) 95%% CI P-value
Death Logistic regression 0.39 0.10 to 1.55 0.181
AKI requiring dialysis Logistic regression 0.94 0.30 to 3.01 0.920
30-day cardiopulmonary readmission Logistic regression 1.12 0.49 to 2.61 0.784

3 Adjusted Lab Analyses

3.1 First-Week Longitudinal

Adjusted First-Week Longitudinal Lab Results
Day-by-day post-operative comparison between treatment groups across the first week
PARAMCD model_type n_subjects n_observations Observed days Placebo mean RBT-1 mean Baseline adjusted Adj. %% difference 95%% CI Treatment P-value Day Interaction P-value
GFRECRT Mixed model, random intercept 416 2602 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 76.78 79.64 Yes 1.4% -2.8% to 5.8% 0.510 0.707
OXYSAT Mixed model, random intercept 407 411 1, 2 94.42 95.57 Yes 0.6% -0.6% to 1.7% 0.322 0.475
LACTICAC Mixed model, random intercept 393 397 1, 2 2.45 2.55 Yes 0.9% -7.6% to 10.1% 0.842 0.500
TROPONI Mixed model, random intercept 348 670 1, 2 5950.26 5619.23 Yes 4.3% -19.9% to 35.7% 0.755 0.130
SCREAT Mixed model, random intercept 416 2602 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 1.03 1.04 Yes -0.7% -2.9% to 1.6% 0.534 0.785

Comment: “Adj. % difference” is the model-estimated average percent difference for RBT-1 versus placebo after adjustment for post-operative day, age, and the corresponding pre-surgery laboratory value, with a random intercept for subject. Positive values indicate higher estimated laboratory values in RBT-1; negative values indicate lower estimated values in RBT-1. Because the model is fit on the log scale, these values are approximate back-transformed percent differences rather than raw arithmetic differences between the observed group means.
